sourceTitle: Use charts to visualize data and track progress directly in Notion

questions.question: Which Notion view type must you select to create a chart from an existing database?

options.text: Table
options.explanation: Table view shows rows and columns but does not create a visual chart.

options.text: Board
options.explanation: Board view provides a kanban layout, not a chart visualization.

options.text: Chart
options.explanation: Choosing the Chart option creates a chart view that can be customized and filtered.

options.text: Calendar
options.explanation: Calendar view displays dates, not chart data.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of a donut chart in Notion?

options.text: Shows trends over time
options.explanation: Trends over time are better represented by line charts.

options.text: Visualizes simple distribution of categories or assignees
options.explanation: Donut charts are ideal for quick distribution views, such as tasks per team member.

options.text: Compares multiple sub‑groups side‑by‑side
options.explanation: Bar charts are used for multi‑dimensional comparisons.

options.text: Displays hierarchical relationships
options.explanation: Hierarchical data isn’t shown with donut charts.

questions.question: How do Notion charts stay up‑to‑date when the underlying data changes?

options.text: You must manually refresh each chart
options.explanation: Charts automatically reflect changes; no manual refresh is needed.

options.text: Charts are static snapshots taken at creation
options.explanation: They are live views, not static images.

options.text: Charts are linked to the database view and update in realtime
options.explanation: Because charts are database views, any edit to the source data updates the chart instantly.

options.text: You need to re‑import the database into the chart
options.explanation: Re‑importing isn’t required; the link is continuous.

questions.question: Which chart type would be most appropriate for tracking the number of pieces published each week over a quarter?

options.text: Donut chart
options.explanation: Donut charts show distribution, not time‑based trends.

options.text: Horizontal bar chart
options.explanation: Bar charts compare categories, but aren’t ideal for continuous time series.

options.text: Line chart
options.explanation: Line charts excel at showing progress over time, such as weekly publishing counts.

options.text: Pie chart
options.explanation: Pie charts are not mentioned in Notion and don’t convey temporal data.

questions.question: If you want to compare OKR status by team and also see bottlenecks in a single view, which combination of chart types is recommended?

options.text: Two donut charts side by side
options.explanation: Donut charts don’t show comparative bottlenecks across teams.

options.text: A vertical bar chart for status by team and a line chart for key‑result progress over time
options.explanation: Bar charts give status distribution, while line charts reveal temporal trends and bottlenecks.

options.text: A single line chart with multiple lines for each team
options.explanation: While possible, it can become cluttered; the guide suggests using both bar and line charts.

options.text: A stacked horizontal bar chart only
options.explanation: Stacked bars show proportion but not time‑based progress.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of embedding an external chart (e.g., from Tableau) into a Notion dashboard?

options.text: It converts the external chart into a Notion database
options.explanation: Embedding displays the chart; it doesn’t transform data into a Notion database.

options.text: It allows you to view both Notion data and external analytics in one place without leaving Notion
options.explanation: Embedding creates a unified view, combining internal and external insights on a single dashboard.

options.text: It syncs the external tool’s data back into Notion automatically
options.explanation: Embedding is visual only; automatic sync isn’t provided.

options.text: It replaces the need for any Notion charts
options.explanation: Embedded charts complement, not replace, native Notion charts.

questions.question: When creating a dashboard that visualizes data from multiple databases, which Notion command can you use to add each chart?

options.text: /table
options.explanation: /table creates a table view, not a chart.

options.text: /link
options.explanation: /link creates a linked database, not a chart view.

options.text: /chart
options.explanation: Typing /chart prompts you to link an existing database and creates a chart view for the dashboard.

options.text: /embed
options.explanation: /embed is for external content, not for creating a Notion chart view.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios best illustrates the use of a synced database turned into a chart?

options.text: Visualizing a static list of company policies stored in Notion
options.explanation: Static lists don’t require syncing from external tools.

options.text: Displaying real‑time Jira tickets alongside Notion tasks in a single chart
options.explanation: Synced databases pull data from tools like Jira; charting them gives a live view of cross‑tool tasks.

options.text: Creating a donut chart of employee birthdays from a Notion HR database
options.explanation: This uses native Notion data; syncing isn’t involved.

options.text: Embedding a Google Analytics line chart into a Notion page
options.explanation: Embedding is external; it doesn’t involve a synced Notion database.

questions.question: How can AI‑generated tags be leveraged together with Notion charts to improve data exploration?

options.text: AI tags replace the need for any charting by automatically summarizing data
options.explanation: AI tags provide metadata; charts still visualize that metadata.

options.text: Charts can visualize the distribution of AI‑generated tags, making large datasets quickly understandable
options.explanation: By charting tag frequencies, teams can explore patterns without manually auditing each row.

options.text: AI tags automatically create bar charts without user input
options.explanation: Charts must still be manually added; AI does not generate charts.

options.text: AI tags are only usable in text fields and cannot be charted
options.explanation: Tags are a property type that can be used in chart axes or filters.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes why a Notion dashboard is considered a "living record" compared to a static spreadsheet chart?

options.text: Because it is exported as a PDF that updates automatically
options.explanation: PDFs are static; they don’t update.

options.text: Because the charts are linked to live database views that refresh in realtime as data changes
options.explanation: The dashboard reflects current data because each chart is a live view of its source database.

options.text: Because Notion dashboards are stored on a separate server that periodically pulls data
options.explanation: Dashboards exist within the Notion workspace and update instantly, not on a schedule.

options.text: Because they only display data that was imported at the time of creation
options.explanation: That would make them static, which contradicts the article’s claim.
